review_text: I thought the movie was flat, and not sure if this was a romance, because there was nothing romantic about it. It was a beautiful movie, Ireland parts were really pretty, but the story, was not for me. I do like the actors, but there was no passion, just a tired story of woman who didn't know her mind and she seemed emotionless. She came across cold, truthfully, I don't think she loved either of the men. Maybe in the 50s it was like that; marriage was more for convenience, not love.
It's a masterpiece, and profoundly moving, especially if you're an immigrant yourself. The closing is one of the most beautiful romantic scenes ever. In my opinion.

Acted superb.no melodrama.just perfection.Miss Ronan taken a bow.Emery Cohen was super.Finally a portrayal of Italian Americans that is honest.no cliché.speaking as a child of Irish Emigrants from the Bronx this rings true.every emotion that Aislish had they had.Trying to be decent and hardworking in a new world while homesickness was ever present.The dinner scene is absolutely perfection.the hand gestures nods etc...

A piece of life. This sentence best describes this movie for me. This movie captures raw human emotions and experiences in a very clever and thoughtful way. Themes like immigration, love, self-doubt, and homesickness are all broken down in the most human way. This movie's only weakness is that it has no tension or promise in the first two acts, which is fine if you are patient because the third act explodes with tension, leading to a most satisfying conclusion.

Brookyln will seem entirely ordinary, but rest assured there is this constant element of refreshing youth and peace from start to finish. Although it's set in old America, this film has such a genuine energy and revitalization that watching it you just can't help but to root for the main characters and finish it as fast as you can. Ronan is the loveliest actress in years, and Cohen does a great job balancing that aw-shucks character with subtle appeal. A pefect movie.

This one really hit me. It’s simple, emotional, and so beautifully made. Saoirse Ronan says more with her eyes than most actors say with full monologues. The movie isn’t loud or dramatic, but the emotions run deep. That feeling of being stuck between two worlds one old and one new it stays with you. Also, it’s probably the most polite love triangle I’ve ever seen. #BrooklynFilm #SaoirseRonan #HeartfeltCinema #DesiWhoLovesDrama

I really liked this one. Was surprised though that the audience score was so high. This is one of those quiet, human experience, type stories that I really get into that audiences don't seem to like as much. Love the period piece feel to it. Really good acting bringing the characters to life. Nostalgic feel for me, not because of the period, but that point of life where you are venturing out, finding your way, finding love. Great movie.

Like Past Lives, this isn’t the typical immigrant story. For one thing, 1951 isn’t the period that comes to mind when considering Irish migration to the U.S. But Eilis (Saoirse Ronan), leaves her picturesque town in Southeast Ireland for opportunity in NYC. Eilis must learn how to travel by boat, about baseball, eat spaghetti, and portray a friendly disposition to sell department store products all while handling mounting homesickness. Besides her sister (Fiona Glascott), Eilis doesn’t have anything else, in particular, she yearns for back in the old country — moreover, she’s in Brooklyn, which is swollen with several generations of Éire countrymen. And yet, there’s just a powerfulness to the concept of “home” that when lacking, is felt so very deeply. Ronan gives a great performance, but something is missing from the film that is undoubtedly flush in the novel, given its popularity and the skill of the author, Colm Tóibín. The story becomes much more interesting once Eilis returns to Ireland, already married — to an Italian-American named “Tony” (Emory Cohen), no less — and is set upon by wolves. Not literally, but the subterranean machinations of (seemingly everybody) back in town to get her to marry a good Irish boy, Jim Farrell (Dohmnall Gleeson). The guilt, the connivance, the cahoots! Perhaps I’m being too harsh on our protag, but her flirting with the idea of remaining in Ireland turned me off to Eilis. Tony didn’t pressure her at all. Tony was a good man. Don’t do this to Tony. Brooklyn had been one of the longest sitters on my list — the song “Goodbye Eilis” from the soundtrack is a must-add for any study/writing playlist and had been on mine several years before watching the movie. The ending was picture-perfect.
Good acting with a human plot about love and self-fulfillment.

Ronan never disappoints, but story is so narrow minded and small town that it's hard to like the movie. There's such a strong pull to keep Eilis in her place, back to Ireland, in the vicious circle of gossip and narrowmindedness that it's a wonder she goes back to America. I thought my hometown was narrow, stilted and backward, but this small town Irish place is just stifling. Read "Long Island", just confirmed how backward and small minded the people were. I hope this is merely a depiction and not the way people really live.

A simple and elegant old fashioned romance that caught me off my guard. Honestly it's all quite touching and somehow manages to avoid anachronisms. The main selling point is Saoirse Ronan, who gives a truly wonderful performance.

Beautifully stunning film. The entire cast was phenomenal, Saoirse Ronan's performance was absolutely remarkable and she's long overdue for an Oscar in my opinion. I'm not really into really into romance movies, but I've watched this film multiple times and it never fails to impress. The story is pure and heartwarming, exploring themes of life, the concept of home, and love. It's moving while not feeling "cheesy" at all. The cinematography is striking, and the humour is great, too. Definitely a must-watch.
A must see period drama.

Cute, charming, and serviceable story about immigrating to the US. Gorgeous production design, costumes, camerawork, and a soundtrack. A compelling 1950s period piece, all things considered. The narrative itself is the prominent melting pot fable where the girl might be Irish, the boy might be Italian, but the kids will be American, and that's the cornerstone of "the greatest nation on Earth," and yada yada yada. Mercifully, the movie doesn't do the last part, or at least it never frames it like that. The choice that Eilis must make isn't as unambiguous. It can be noted how the boys are practically allegorical representations of their countries. And how the movie facetiously highlights how different they are, from appearance to the simple fact that one likes to listen and the other prefers talking. Aside from a very melodramatic tipping point that compels Eilis to finally choose her future, the movie doesn't present some type of harmonious answer to her crisis. No, unfortunately, there can't be a happy ending for them all, and that tangible sacrifice that she follows through with makes the story all the more touching.
Absolutely stunning, you must see it
Brooklyn (2015) is for the most part just a great movie with great performances

Dramma di formazione, romantico ed elegante; che sembra costruito come un vestito su misura per la sua musa Saoirse Ronan, semplicemente perfetta in questo tipo di ruoli. La Ronan riesce ad emozionare e a creare empatia con estrema semplicità, portando il suo personaggio nella quotidianità e nella complessità della vita; così come sarà in futuro anche per la sua Lady Bird. La sceneggiatura di qualità riesce a sostenere un numero ristretto di grandi eventi, riempiendo tutto con un'ottima rappresentazione delle emozioni e delle piccole situazioni che si creano nelle giornate della protagonista. Molto ben riuscito anche il finale.
Not my style but I still immensely enjoyed it.
A young woman's life in Ireland and New York in the 1950s.
Beautiful film about home, as well as about homes away from home. Makes 1950s Brooklyn come alive, which is some feat since the film was primarily shot in Montreal. Saoirse Ronan is stunning.
This movie treats homessickness as a real condition... and I think it's contagious.

Strong language at the beginning of the movie was unnecessary and jarring. The male lead in the movie was shorter than the female lead. She was pressured into marriage before she returned to Ireland. I don't think that based on her character and values that she would have agreed to that. However, she did and once in Ireland she should have told everyone that she was a married woman. It is a flaw in her character that she did not. The story would have been Academy Award material if she arrived back in her homeland a single woman. Nevertheless, this was an above average movie that was well done mostly and entertaining. It took us on a journey for 2 hours.

This was really nicely done. Loved the costumes, acting was very good but most of all how it captured the feeling of....immigrating and starting over from scratch somewhere new. Not really fitting in, the home sickness and seeing the old home in a new way, how others see you there, the judgment and expectations. Do you still fit there or in the new place?

Saoirse Ronan!!!! Is there a performance that she cannot have you love! a relatable American Story and really a Global story during a particular time in History. a wonderful story one that you hear often from ones that live within the United states. the Acting on Saoirse Part was wonderful!
For such a simple plot, it really is emotionally affecting. A really sweet film with a lot of heart and rawness to the emotions. Will make you homesick just watching it.
Simple yet amazing story about love,commitment and specially on life of people who live away from home to work and get a better life by making sacrifice.

"Brooklyn" is not a great movie, but there are a lot of positive things to say about it. The movie is very well made and the cast does very well in their respective roles. Saoirse Ronan is very good in her Academy Award nominated performance. The movie's locations help add to the movie's genuine feel. The cast does not appear to be acting on studio sets. It feels very genuine. Nick Hornby also did a very good job with the screen adaptation. With a few minor changes the movie is very faithful to Colm Toibin's novel of the same title upon which the movie is based. Basically it is the total opposite of the terrible 2018 film adaption of "Annihilation" starring Academy Award winner Natalie Portman. There were a few times I had difficulty understanding Jim Broadbent because he is very soft spoken in the movie and talks rather fast at times. That is really the only criticism I have of the movie for the most part. If you decide to see "Brooklyn" you are not in for a truly great movie, but rather one that is very entertaining and a movie I think you will enjoy.
